skill.name: geo-database
skill.description: Access NCBI GEO guidance for finding, downloading, and analyzing public gene expression datasets from GSE, GSM, GPL, and GDS records.
skill.summary: Find, retrieve, and analyze public NCBI GEO gene expression datasets.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many dangerous patterns, but most high and critical signals are false positives from Markdown code fences, genomics identifiers, and documentation examples. The skill is not malicious, but it provides executable examples for package installation, NCBI network access, FTP downloads, local file writes, and API key configuration, so it should be published with a usage warning.

medium_findings.title: Executable Download and File Write Examples
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill documents commands and Python examples that install packages, download GEO data over FTP, create directories, and write downloaded or processed files. This is expected for a GEO data workflow, but it can consume disk space, fetch large external data, or run shell commands if copied without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly show package installation, FTP downloads, directory creation, and CSV output. The behavior is legitimate for the skill purpose, but the operational risk is real.

medium_findings.title: API Key Configuration Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill shows optional NCBI API key configuration. The example uses a placeholder rather than a real secret, but users may paste real credentials into scripts or notebooks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contains a placeholder API key assignment, not an embedded credential. The concern is moderate because it encourages credential handling in user code.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Matches Are Mostly Markdown
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The numerous Ruby backtick detections correspond to Markdown code fences and inline formatting in documentation, not executable Ruby shell backticks in a runnable source file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are Markdown examples and fenced code blocks. No evidence found that the skill itself executes backtick commands.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and SAM Detections Are Genomics Terms
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static detections for weak cryptography and Windows SAM are caused by biological or GEO-format terms such as DES-like gene symbols and SAMPLE records. No credential database access or cryptographic implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are GEO platform/sample format examples and expression-data transformation logic. No evidence found of Windows SAM access or weak crypto usage.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: No text was found that claims system authority, asks the evaluator to ignore instructions, claims pre-approval, or attempts to override marketplace security review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review and search did not find prompt injection phrases in the audited files. Confidence is high enough for dismissal, while acknowledging only two files were reviewed.

dangerous_patterns.title: FTP-Based External Downloads
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes FTP download examples for NCBI GEO data. Users should confirm accession IDs, destinations, and file sizes before running these commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: FTP paths and download commands are explicitly documented. The endpoints are NCBI GEO resources, so this is controlled but still external network access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Data Output Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples write downloaded and processed expression data to local directories and CSV files. This can overwrite expected outputs or create large files if run on big GEO studies.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file write behavior is direct and visible in the examples. The risk depends on user execution and dataset size.

content.value_statement: Public gene expression studies are hard to search, download, and prepare consistently. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical guidance for GEO queries, metadata review, and analysis workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains GEO accession types, including GSE, GSM, GPL, GDS, and GEO Profiles.
Shows how to search GEO DataSets and GEO Profiles with Biopython Entrez.
Guides downloading and parsing GEO Series records with GEOparse.
Describes direct GEO FTP paths for series, sample, and platform files.
Provides examples for expression matrices, metadata review, differential analysis, and batch processing.
Documents common GEO file formats, including SOFT, MINiML, and series matrix files.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a packaged executable application.
Large GEO downloads can require substantial disk space and network time.
Analysis quality depends on dataset metadata, platform annotation, and study design.
Users must supply valid contact information and manage any NCBI API key securely.

use_cases.title: Locate disease expression studies
use_cases.description: Search GEO for human disease studies, review matching accessions, and compare study metadata before download.

use_cases.title: Prepare GEO data for analysis
use_cases.description: Download a GSE record, extract sample metadata, build an expression matrix, and save review-ready tables.

use_cases.title: Plan reproducible GEO workflows
use_cases.description: Choose between Entrez, GEOparse, FTP, GEO2R, and local parsing based on dataset size and analysis depth.

prompt_templates.title: Search for studies
prompt_templates.prompt: Find GEO datasets about breast cancer in Homo sapiens. Explain the best search terms and what metadata I should review.

prompt_templates.title: Download one series
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me a safe workflow to download GSE123456 with GEOparse, inspect sample metadata, and avoid unnecessary supplementary files.

prompt_templates.title: Compare sample groups
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me split samples in a GEO series into control and treatment groups, then outline differential expression checks.

prompt_templates.title: Design a batch workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a reproducible batch workflow for several GSE studies, including metadata validation, caching, rate limits, and output tables.

output_examples.input: Search GEO for human RNA-seq studies related to diabetes.
output_examples.output: A ranked query plan with organism filters, study-type terms, accession review steps, and metadata fields to check before download.

output_examples.input: Review a GSE accession before downloading supplementary files.
output: Summary of study title, organism, sample count, platform, and available file types.
A recommendation on whether the series matrix, SOFT file, or supplementary files are most appropriate.

output_examples.input: Plan analysis for TP53 across multiple GEO studies.
output_examples.output: A workflow covering platform annotation, probe-to-gene mapping, sample grouping, expression extraction, and cross-study comparability checks.

best_practices: Set a valid Entrez email and respect NCBI rate limits for automated requests.
Check file sizes and destination paths before downloading supplementary or raw files.
Review sample metadata and platform annotations before interpreting expression results.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real API keys into shared notebooks, prompts, or repository files.
Do not run recursive FTP downloads without confirming scope and available disk space.
Do not compare expression values across platforms without annotation and batch-effect review.

faq.question: Does this skill download GEO data by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides instructions and examples that a user or agent may run after review.

faq.question: Which GEO accessions does it cover?
faq.answer: It explains GSE series, GSM samples, GPL platforms, GDS datasets, and GEO Profiles.

faq.question: Can it help with RNA-seq and microarray data?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance covers both sequence-based studies and array-based expression studies.

faq.question: Does it require an NCBI API key?
faq.answer: No. An API key is optional for higher E-utilities rate limits, but an Entrez email is expected.

faq.question: What tools are referenced?
faq.answer: The skill references Biopython Entrez, GEOparse, pandas, GEO FTP, and GEO2R workflows.

faq.question: What should users verify before running examples?
faq.answer: Users should verify accession IDs, download size, output paths, rate limits, and credential handling.
